text: Thank you. Good afternoon and welcome to Meta Platform's First Quarter, 2026 Earnings Conference
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: Call. Joining me today to discuss our results are Mark Zuckerberg, CEO and Susan Lee, CFO.
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: Our remarks today will include forward-looking statements which are based on assumptions as of
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: today. Actual results may differ materially as a result of various factors, including those set
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: forth in today's earnings press release and in our annual report on Form 10K filed with the SEC.
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: We undertake new obligation to update any forward-looking statement. During this call,
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: we will present both GAAP and certain non-GAAP financial measures. A reconciliation of GAAP
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: to non-GAAP measures is included in today's earnings press release. The earnings press release
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: and an accompanying investor presentation are available on our website at investor.atmeta.com
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: and now I'd like to turn the call over to Mark. All right. Hey everyone. Thanks for joining today.
speaker: KENNETH DORELL - Director of Investor Relations

text: We had a strong quarter for our community, our business and our progress towards AI.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: More than 3.5 billion people use at least one of our apps every day. We saw a small decrease in
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: total family dailies due to internet outages in Iran and blocks in Russia, but otherwise
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: trends across our apps are strong. Daily and monthly activities on Instagram and Facebook
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: continue to grow with video driving all-time high engagement across both apps. What's app
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: continues to see strong momentum too, including in the US, and threads continues on its trajectory
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: to be the leading app in its category. Our biggest milestone so far this year has been the
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: release of our Muse family of models and our first model, Muse Spark, along with a significantly
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: upgraded new version of Meta AI. This was the first release from Meta Superintelligence Labs,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: and it shows that our work is on track to build a leading lab. Over the past 10 months, we have
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: built the strongest research team in the industry and established the scientific and
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: technical foundations to scale very advanced models. Spark is just one step on that scaling
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: ladder, and we are already training even more advanced models. But Spark has already made Meta AI
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: a world-class assistant that leads in several areas related to our vision of personal super
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: intelligence, including visual understanding, health, shopping, social content, local,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: creating games, and more. We're hearing very positive feedback on it so far. We've seen large
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: increases in Meta AI use since releasing the updates, and the Meta AI app has consistently
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: been near the top of the app stores as well. Now that we have a strong model, we can develop
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: more novel products as well. Since I first wrote about our vision for personal super intelligence
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: last year, we've been focused on delivering personal and business agents to billions of people
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: around the world. Our goal is not just to deliver Meta AI as an assistant, but to deliver agents
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: that can understand your goals and then work day and night to help you achieve them. My view of AI
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: is very different from many others in the industry. I hear a lot of people out there talk about how
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: AI is going to replace people. Instead, I think that AI is going to amplify people's ability to
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: do what you want, whether that's to improve your health, your learning, your relationships,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: your ability to achieve your personal career goals, and more. My view is that human progress has
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: always been driven by people pursuing their individual aspirations, and I believe that this
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: will continue to be true in the future. People will be more important in the future, not less.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Meta believes in empowering individuals, and those are the kinds of products that we're going to
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: build, and I believe that they're going to be some of the most important and valuable products of all
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: time. We are building a personal agent focused on helping people achieve the diverse goals in their
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: lives. We're also building a business agent focused on helping entrepreneurs and businesses across
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: the world use our tools and others to grow their efforts, reach new customers, and serve existing
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: customers better. These agents will work together to form an ecosystem, and whether you use our
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: personal or business agents to achieve your goals, I believe that the future will see a massive
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: increase in entrepreneurship from people creating new things that they've always wanted to exist,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: but previously didn't have the tools to bring into the world. We're already testing an early
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: version of business AIs and weekly conversations have grown 10x since the start of this year.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: We're also working on using Spark and our upcoming models to improve our recommendation systems and
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: core business and Facebook, Instagram, and ads. Right now, our apps primarily help people accomplish
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: three important goals, connecting with people, learning about the world, and entertainment.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: But we've always wanted our apps to understand more of people's goals so we can help improve their
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: lives in all the ways that they want. These new AI models will let us understand this in more detail,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: so instead of just looking at statistical patterns of what types of people engage with what content,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: for the first time in meta's history, we're going to be able to develop a first principles
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: understanding of what you care about and what each piece of content in our system is about,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: so that way we can show you more useful things for what you're trying to accomplish. And we'll
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: also be able to create personalized content specifically for people to help you achieve your
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: goals as well. Since our recommendation systems are operating at such large scale,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: we'll phase in this new research and technology over time. But the trend over the last few years
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: seems clear that we are seeing an increasing return on the amount that we can improve engagement
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: for people and value for advertisers. This encourages us to continue investing heavily in what we expect
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: will provide increasing value over the coming years as well. On that note, we are increasing
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: our infrastructure CapEx forecast for this year. Most of that is due to higher component costs,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: particularly memory pricing. But every sign that we're seeing in our own work and across the industry
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: gives us confidence in this investment. That said, we are very focused on increasing the
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: efficiency of our investments. And as part of that, we are rolling out more than one gigawatt
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: of our own custom silicon that we're developing with Broadcom, as well as significant amount of
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: AMD chips to complement the new NVIDIA systems that we're rolling out as well.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: One of the primary goals of our Metacomput initiative is to lead the industry in efficiency
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: of building compute. And we expect that will be a strategic advantage over time. Talking about
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: building physical goods at scale, our AI glasses continue to perform well with the number of people
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: using them, daily tripling year over year. This continues to be one of the fastest growing categories
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: of consumer electronics ever. We released Rayban meta-optics this quarter, designed for all day
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: where rather than primarily as sunglasses. And building on our release of Oakley last year,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: we have some exciting new partnerships and styles that I think are going to have the potential to
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: reach even more people coming later this year. All of our glasses are designed to easily update,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: to use our newest AI models and features. I'm also really excited to see the glasses evolve
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: from being able to answer questions to being able to be a personal agent that's with you all
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: day long, helping you remember things and achieve your goals. Beyond glasses, I am excited for more
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: of our metaverse efforts to be powered by the AI models we're training as well. We remain the
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: biggest investors in the VR space across the industry, but we are focused on making our VR
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: business sustainable as we invest more in other areas like AI and glasses. Before wrapping,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: I want to talk for a moment about how AI is transforming our work. We are seeing more and
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: more examples where one or two people are building something in a week that would have previously
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: taken dozens of people months. And I want to make sure that meta is the best place in the world for
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: these types of people to come and make an impact. We're building the next evolution of our company
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: around these people. And there's a lot that we can do to enable this. Building the best infrastructure
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: for creating and delivering products at scale, streamlining our teams so they aren't bigger than
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: they need to be, recognizing and rewarding the people who are having outsized impacts,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: and setting ourselves up to try many more ideas and take on many new projects in the future.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Of course, we will continue pushing to increase our efficiency as well, but overall, I think the
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: future is about building many more higher quality things than we've ever built before.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: All right, that is what I wanted to cover today. We are living through a historic technological
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: transformation. We are among the few companies positioned to shape the future, and we are on
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: track to do that. I'm looking forward to delivering personal super intelligence to billions of people.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And as always, I am grateful for the hard work of our teams and to all of you for being on this journey
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: with us. Thanks, Mark. And good afternoon, everyone. Let's begin with our segment results.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: All comparisons are on a year-over-year basis unless otherwise noted. We estimate 3.56 billion
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: people used at least one of our family of apps on a daily basis in March, which declined slightly
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: from December due to internet disruptions in Iran and the restriction on access to WhatsApp and Russia.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Absent these impacts, growth in family daily active people would have been positive quarter over quarter.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Q1 total family of apps revenue was $55.9 billion, up 33% year-over-year. Q1 family of apps add
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: revenue was $55 billion, up 33% or 29% on a constant currency basis. In Q1, the total number of ad
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: impressions served across our services increased 19%. Impression growth was healthy across all
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: regions driven primarily by growth in engagement and users as well as ad load optimizations.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: The global average price per ad increased 12% year-over-year in Q1 with broad based growth as we
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: benefited from ad performance improvements, better macro conditions versus Q1 of last year, and currency
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: tailwinds in international regions. This was partially offset by strong impression growth,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: including from lower monetizing regions. Family of apps other revenue was $885 million,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: up 74%, driven primarily by WhatsApp paid messaging and subscriptions revenue. Within our reality
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: lab segment, Q1 revenue was $402 million, down 2% year-over-year due to lower quest headset sales,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: which were partially offset by continued strong growth in AI glasses revenue.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Moving now to our consolidated results. Q1 total revenue was $56.3 billion, up 33% or 29% on a
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: constant currency basis. Q1 total expenses were $33.4 billion, up 35% compared to last year.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Year-over-year growth was driven mainly by infrastructure costs and employee compensation.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: The growth in infrastructure costs was due to higher depreciation, data center operating costs,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: and third-party cloud spend. The growth in employee compensation was driven by technical
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: hires we've added over the past year, particularly AI talent. We ended Q1 with over 77,900 employees,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: down 1% from Q4 as the impact of headcount optimization efforts in certain functions was
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: partially offset by hiring and priority areas of monetization and infrastructure.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: First quarter operating income was $22.9 billion, representing a 41% operating margin.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Q1 interest in other income was negative $1.1 billion, driven by unrealized losses on our equity
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: investments. Our tax rate for the quarter was negative 23%, which was favorably impacted by a
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: tax benefit of $8.03 billion. This benefit partially relieves the $15.93 billion non-cash
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: tax charge we recorded in the third quarter of 2025, which reflects updated guidance from the
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: U.S. Treasury issued in February 2026 regarding the tax treatment of previously capitalized R&D
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: expenditures in the United States. Absent the tax benefit, our Q1 tax rate would have been
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: 14%. Net income was $26.8 billion or $10.44 per share. Absent the tax benefit, our net income and EPS
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: would have been $18.7 billion and $7.31 respectively. Capital expenditures, including principal payments
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: on finance leases, were $19.8 billion, driven by investments in servers, data centers, and network
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: infrastructure. Free cash flow was $12.4 billion. We ended the quarter with $81.2 billion in cash
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: and marketable securities and $58.7 billion in debt. Turning now to the business performance,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: there are two primary factors that drive our revenue performance, our ability to deliver
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: engaging experiences for our community, and our effectiveness at monetizing that engagement over
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: time. On the first, we're continuing to see significant gains from our content recommendation
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: initiatives. On Instagram, the ranking improvements that we made in Q1 drove a 10% lift in real-time
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: spent. On Facebook, total video time increased more than 8% globally in Q1, the largest quarter
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: over quarter gain in four years. Within the U.S. and Canada, ranking improvements we made
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: drove a 9% increase in video watch time on Facebook and Q1. These gains are benefiting from
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: advances we're making across the full stack. Starting with data, we doubled the length of
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: user interaction sequences we use for training on Instagram and Q1 and increased the richness of
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: how each user interaction is described, enabling our systems to develop a deeper understanding of
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: user interests. Within our models, we've significantly increased the speed with which our ranking
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: models index new posts, which is enabling us to recommend them sooner after they are published.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: We're also applying more advanced content understanding techniques, which is enabling us to quickly
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: identify posts that may be interesting to someone, even if they haven't engaged with a lot of similar
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: content. These and other improvements have enabled us to increase the diversity and
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: recency of recommended content with same day posts now representing more than 30% of recommended
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: reels on both Instagram and Facebook, more than double the levels one year ago. We're also using
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: AI to unlock more inventory by auto translating and dubbing videos into a viewer's local language,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: enabling us to recommend a more diverse set of content. Over half a billion users on each of
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Facebook and Instagram are now watching AI translated videos weekly. Looking forward,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: we're making several investments we expect will deliver more valuable recommendations.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: This year, we will continue scaling up our models in several dimensions, including their size and
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: complexity, while incorporating LOMs to deepen content understanding across our platform.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: This will enable us to better match people to a wider variety of content aligned to their interests.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: At the same time, we are executing on our longer term efforts to develop the next generation of
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: our recommendation systems. This includes building foundation models that power organic content
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: and adds recommendations, as well as developing LOM based recommender systems. Our focus this
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: year is validating the model architectures and techniques in these domains before we scale them
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: out in future years. Aside from our recommendations work, we are focused on deploying the models
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: from meta super intelligence labs to enable a new set of product experiences. We're seeing
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: encouraging results within meta AI since we began powering responses with the first model from MSL,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Muse Spark. In tests we ran leading up to the launch, we saw meaningful engagement gains that
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: accelerated week over week with each new iteration of the model. We're seeing similar
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: gains within meta AI following the broad rollout of our new model with double digit percent increases
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: in meta AI sessions per user. Muse Spark is now powering meta AI in direct chat threads across
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: our family of apps, as well as the standalone meta AI app and website, giving billions of people
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: globally access to our latest model. Overall, we're very encouraged by the momentum within our
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: research and product roadmap and look forward to sharing more detail on what we're building
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: over the course of the year. Turning to the second driver of our revenue performance,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: increasing monetization efficiency. The first part of this work is optimizing the level of
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: ads within organic engagement. Here we continue to enhance our systems to show ads at the optimal
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: time and location. In Q1, we also expanded availability of ads on our newer surfaces,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: including bringing ads on threads to people in more markets. On WhatsApp, we're making good
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: progress with the rollout of ads and status with hundreds of millions of people now viewing them
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: daily. Moving to the second part of increasing monetization efficiency, improving performance
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: for the businesses who use our services. To do so, we're deploying AI more deeply across
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: each layer of our systems and tools. Within our ad systems, we're delivering performance gains
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: as we deploy more complex and predictive models. In Q1, enhancements we made to lattices modeling
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: and learning techniques along with advances in our gem model architecture drove a more than 6%
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: increase in conversion rate for landing page view ads. In addition, we've been investing in
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: more performant inference models for when we're serving ads. In the second half of last year,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: we began rolling out our new adaptive ranking model, which is an LLM scale ads recommender
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: model that we use for inference. This model improves our inference ROI by routing requests to more
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: compute intensive inference models when it determines there is a higher probability of conversion.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: In Q1, we expanded coverage of our adaptive ranking model to support offsite conversions,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: which drove a 1.6% increase in conversion rates across the major services on Facebook and Instagram.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: We're also leveraging AI to make it easier for businesses to manage their customers,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: develop ad creative and engage with customers. The meta AI business assistant has now been fully
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: rolled out to all eligible advertisers on supported meta buying services, providing personalized
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: recommendations to advertisers, resolving account issues, and servicing campaign insights to help
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: optimize results. Performance has been strong since we began testing the assistant in Q4,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: with common account issues being resolved at a 20% higher rate. This week, we're also introducing
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: meta ads AI connectors in open beta, providing advertisers the ability to connect their meta
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: ad account directly to an AI agent. We've always supported advertisers both on our platform and
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: through tools like the marketing API. And now we're extending that to AI so businesses and
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: agencies can analyze and optimize campaigns with the tools they're already using.

text: advertisers. These tools are benefiting performance as well, with advertisers using our video generation

text: feature seeing more than 3% higher conversion rates in tests. We're also seeing good traction

text: than 10 million conversations each week being facilitated through business AIs up from 1 million

text: at the start of the year. We'll further expand access to more countries this quarter while adding

text: more capabilities to the AIs. We also continue to invest in the value optimization suite,

text: which helps advertisers maximize their return on ad spend by prioritizing the highest value

text: conversions rather than optimizing solely for the most conversions at the lowest cost. Adoption

text: by businesses has been strong following performance improvements we've made over the past year,

text: to 10 billion dollars. To support the product discovery and purchasing happening through creators,

text: we're expanding our solutions beyond ads. Last month we rolled out our affiliate partnerships

text: retailers on their posts and earn a commission when someone makes a purchase. We have also started

text: discover and buy products within our services, particularly as we incorporate AI deeply across

text: our platforms. Next I would like to discuss our approach to capital allocation. Compute is
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: becoming increasingly important as it determines the quality of services we can provide, including
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: powering more capable models and delivering innovative new products. It is also becoming
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: more critical to how we work at Metah. As we are entering a world where employees are managing

speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Scales where the more you want to get out of it, I think people are going to also be willing to pay a lot of money to have premium or high compute versions of it. So I think that this is like it's a very exciting area.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: But I think what you all should be waiting to see is like whether we can build the version that really like just works and how effective we are at converting people who are using our products into being hundreds of millions and then billions of people in the world.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And then we're using this stuff. And then over time, how can we effectively convert that into something that's increasingly profitable by monetizing it and getting the cost down.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: So that's the roadmap of what we need to do.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: You asked about.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: About whether we're primarily focused on consumers or also recursive self improvement.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: I think that you know we've talked about two main goals for the team. I mean, one is this kind of agents version vision of what we're doing.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: The other is that self improvement is really important because you can't build a leading AI product if you don't have leading models.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: So, and you're not going to have leading models in the future if your models can't improve themselves. Right. It's like you're getting to a point where today the models are still able to learn from people.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And then I think at some point the models will have to improve themselves. And that's how how the growth is going to an improvement in the models is going to happen.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And if you don't if we don't have an ability to do that, then we or anyone else I think that the companies that don't do that are not going to be leading labs and they're not going to produce leading product.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: So I know that's like that is a table stakes thing that we are focused on.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Now does that make us a developer tools company? Not necessarily. I mean, I'm not not against having an API or coding tools or anything like that, but it's not our primary focus.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: I actually think people can flate coding with self improvement more than they should. Coding is one ingredient for the model self improving. It's not the only thing.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And we are focused on all of the parts that are going to be necessary for self improvement in service of the personal super intelligent vision that we have for people and businesses.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Your next question comes from the line of Ron Josie with city group. Please go ahead.

speaker: RONALD JOSEY - Analyst, Citigroup

There's a thesis out there that may be some of the ranking recommendation improvements are are long in the two. So it seems as if there's a lot more room to go. So any help any help there be helpful. Thank you.
speaker: RONALD JOSEY - Analyst, Citigroup

text: I mean, I think that the agents work. There's going to be short term versions of it. But then I think that there's going to be massive upside for delivering more intelligence and more capabilities in the models.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And you're kind of seeing this across the industry each, you know, month. You know, each generation of models. They just have more capabilities and can do more things and people absorb it and are able to get more superpowers and it's awesome.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: It's like the most exciting time in the industry. So I think of the agents as the product vehicle for delivering that capability to people.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And we certainly, you know, I think this year is going to be a, um, a, a key period for establishing that as the vehicle for how people are going to use this. But then the model improvement, I think, is going to be something that's going to go on for a very long time.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: So there's a lot to do here in both the short, medium and long term.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And then on your second question, which I think is about the ranking and recommendations improvements that we talked about in our, that I talked about in my earlier remarks.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: You know, I think they're, you know, first of all, there is still a lot of room to continue improving recommendations over the rest of the year.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: And we expect we'll be able to do that to drive additional engagement on both Facebook and Instagram.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: You know, a couple of the things first, we're going to continue to improve our data infrastructure. That's going to allow our models to train on more data.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: And we're adding more detail to how we describe the content that users have engaged with in the past and scaling up the complexity of our model architecture to take advantage of those larger data sets,
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: like using even longer histories of content interactions. And that should all be in service of improving the overall quality of recommendations.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: You know, we also are focused on making the recommendations even more personalized and more relevant to any given user's interest.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: There's work we're doing to redesign our content retrieval system to show more content that matches the full range of a user's interests and to tailor the diversity of the topics we recommend.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: To the broadness of someone's interest. So someone with particularly concentrated interest might see relatively more of that content, while people with a broader set of interest might see kind of a greater range in the topics that we show them.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: And then finally we're continuing to make improvements to our sort of LLM based to neural algorithm features that allow users to provide more granular natural language feedback on what they want to see more of or less of in their feed.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: So the sort of the, the, the kind of the sequence line, which is the thing that you called out is one of really many improvements we made in Q1 and there is a big roadmap of further improvements going forward.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Your next question comes from the line of Doug and move with JP Morgan. Please go ahead.
speaker: OPERATOR - Krista

text: Any questions.
speaker: DOUGLAS ANMUTH - Analyst, JPMorgan

text: Mark, how do you think about the step up as you go from leveraging smaller models in the ad business to use spark and future.
speaker: DOUGLAS ANMUTH - Analyst, JPMorgan

text: Large language models going forward where some of the key unlocks across engagement and monetization and then.
speaker: DOUGLAS ANMUTH - Analyst, JPMorgan

speaker: DOUGLAS ANMUTH - Analyst, JPMorgan

text: I'll take that question on Manus. We're still working through the details. So we don't have an update right now on your first question, which is about sort of the going from leveraging smaller ads businesses smaller models in the ads business to kind of to the ads sort of models growing.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: There, there's already a some work underway and I think I alluded to some of this in my earlier remarks, even kind of in the current landscape of the ads roadmap where we're basically trying to advance the architecture here to allow sort of to allow us to leverage the abilities of
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: the larger models.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Historically, we haven't used larger model architectures like gem for inference because their size and complexity would make them to cost prohibitive and the way we drive performance from those models is by using them to transfer knowledge to smaller, more lightweight models that are used at runtime.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: The inference models are bound by strict latency requirements since they need to find the right ad within milliseconds and that has again historically prevented us from meaningfully sizing up scaling up their size and complexity.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: But in the second half of last year we introduced a new adaptive ranking model which enables us to leverage LLM scale model complexity of a trillion parameters and we made advances in the model architecture.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: We designed the system with the underlying silicon so it maintains the sub second speed that is required to serve ads at scale.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: We also developed an approach that intelligently routes request more compute intensive inference models if it determines that there is a higher probability of conversion and that lets us drive both better performance and increase and increase.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: So there's a lot of work being done there before we even sort of incorporate more of the LLM work into our underlying ads ranking models.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: Yeah, so I might give you a somewhat loftier answer to the question.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: You're asking about shopping. I think it's sort of an interesting.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Example of the way in which the work that we're doing is different.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Then what I think others are doing out there.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: You know these products they.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: A.I. agents get better when you fully optimize the stack.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: That's why we believe that we need to be a company that builds frontier models in addition to building the agents.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And then in order to do that you of course need to build your infrastructure in order to build to do that well.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: So we're undertaking this large investment to be able to do that top to bottom.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And I think a lot of the way to think about the investment that we're making.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Is a bet that.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: The individual things that people care about and that people are going to be more important in the future.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And that's sort of like a and I think it should be a pretty obvious thing to say.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: But I think so much of the rhetoric around a I in the industry is around.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Like a company trying to build some kind of centralized thing that like does all the productive work in society in some way or something like that.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And that just is very different from how we see the world.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: Like our our vision for the future is one where society makes progress by individuals pursuing their own aspirations.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And some people care about big grand things like curing diseases and a lot of people care about personal things like finding the right shirt for my daughter.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And I just think that we want to we're going to build things that help deliver this vision for personal agents for people.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And I think that part of the lane and what is interesting and differentiated about what we're doing is that that's just so different from how I hear everyone else talking about the work that we're doing.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: So even though I think some of these ideas they seem like they should be so obvious.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: I actually think that our approach of trying to empower individuals and building consumer things is just in the details extremely different from what others are doing.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And shopping might be one kind of specific example that I think is going to have interest in commercial implications and I think people consumers are going to like it.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: But, you know, I don't hear any other labs out there talking about how they're building an AI that's really good at shopping.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And I think that the reason for that is like not because shopping is the most important thing by itself, but because like empowering people to do the things that matter in their lives, whether that's local or understanding social context, or, or shopping or personal health things or understanding what's going on around them visually,
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: which is going to be really important on the glasses. These are all elements of the personal super intelligence vision.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And I think like a lot of this and when you're thinking about kind of the investment in meta over time, I think you should think about it as coming down to these set of values around what do we want AI to do in society.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: And if what you want it to do is empower individuals and build a world where the AI is in service to individuals goals, then that is what we are going to build and I think it's going to be incredibly valuable.
speaker: MARK ZUCKERBERG - CEO

text: I almost wish we could end on that answer, but I will answer the second question, which I think kind of as two versions. One is, you know, a version of like, you know, what's the revenue outlook and obviously we gave, you know, the key to guide, which embeds, I think, both a range of kind of macro outcomes, but also the work that we've, you know, the, the ongoing work that we have to continue improving both
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: the sort of usage and engagement on our family of apps and then, you know, our ability to continue making the ads better and more performant.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: You know, I think the second question is maybe more of a, the second version of that question is more of a higher level question about kind of the overall trajectory of the roadmap here.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: And one of the things I, you know, we'll say having been working on this for a very long time is I'm always really impressed by the team's ability to to continue to advance the state of the art here.
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: growth in future years. And that's something that is both quite dialed in and I think that, you know, our ability to measure the impact of that has been pretty robust and it's, you know, been a very important driver of, of our ads revenue growth
speaker: SUSAN LI - CFO

text: and that continues to be a process that, you know, again, we ran in this past budget and as you, and as far as we can, you know, as we have line of sight we feel good about the investment opportunities ahead of us.
